content:
Hi there! I've been chatting with one of the authors of perf and there is some interest in using Rust :crab: While this is a little different to the main focus of Rust for Linux - this is a userspace tool - it does live in the kernel tree.

I've started work on a proof of concept but I'm at the point where I need uapi bindings. I could make my own with bindgen, but since we're in the same source tree, is there a way I can easily use the bindings from rust/uapi from my Cargo project?
I think you may be better of with a separate bindgen invocation. You may be able to get it to work, but it sounds less fragile to not reuse it.

For cargo projects that can use crates.io there is https://github.com/sunfishcode/linux-raw-sys/, but given that you probably don't want to use crates.io and it contains pre-generated bindings rather than building them on the fly from the local kernel source it is probably not a viable option for perf.

but given that you probably don't want to use crates.io

I'll be using crates.io :sweat_smile: perf already has many external C deps, adding a few external Rust deps doesn't seem too bad.

but yeah I think generating bindings from the local kernel source is a better idea.
Just curious, but what do you want to use rust for in perf?

perf already has many external C deps, adding a few external Rust deps doesn't seem too bad.

External deps are fine, but using Cargo for downloading them on the fly might not be, depending on who you ask...

Just curious, but what do you want to use rust for in perf?

memory safety

For the kernel side, to workaround that issue, we are going to provide a way to fetch Rust dependencies from the distribution's registry. So perhaps you can mimic something like that when we have it (and meanwhile start with the normal Cargo stuff). I would at least make sure you use --locked just in case.
i'm just at the PoC stage right now... but i'll be sending this to both the perf list and rust for linux lists for comments/review once i've got something meaningful implemented
